questions.question: Why does React Native Reanimated 2 produce smoother animations than the original Animated API, even when the JavaScript thread is busy?
options: Reanimated 2 uses GPU shaders for all transformations, bypassing both JS and UI threads.

Reanimated 2 compiles animation logic into worklets that execute on the UI thread using JSI (JavaScript Interface), completely bypassing the React bridge. The original Animated API runs on the JS thread — when JS is busy (network responses, re-renders), animation frames are dropped. Reanimated worklets execute independent of JS thread load, guaranteeing 60fps even during JS-intensive operations.
Reanimated 2 uses hardware interpolation while Animated uses software easing.
Reanimated 2 batches animation updates and applies them in a single native call.
questions.answer: Reanimated 2 compiles animation logic into worklets that execute on the UI thread using JSI (JavaScript Interface), completely bypassing the React bridge. The original Animated API runs on the JS thread — when JS is busy (network responses, re-renders), animation frames are dropped. Reanimated worklets execute independent of JS thread load, guaranteeing 60fps even during JS-intensive operations.
questions.explanation: Test case: an accordion animation that plays while a FlatList renders 200 new items. Animated API: accordion animation drops to ~12fps during the list render. Reanimated 2: accordion continues at 60fps — the UI thread (where Reanimated runs) is unaffected by the JS thread work of rendering list items.

questions.question: In useAnimatedStyle, the style computation function runs on the UI thread. What is a critical restriction this places on the code inside useAnimatedStyle?
options: useAnimatedStyle cannot access React state — it can only read shared values.

The worklet (function) inside useAnimatedStyle runs on the UI thread, which has no access to the JS runtime or React state. You CANNOT call JS-thread functions, access React hooks (useState, useContext), or console.log from inside useAnimatedStyle. You can ONLY: read shared values (.value), perform math, use Reanimated utilities (interpolate, clamp). Accessing regular React state from useAnimatedStyle causes a crash or unexpected behavior.
useAnimatedStyle cannot handle conditional animations — only linear value mappings.
The worklet inside useAnimatedStyle must be a pure function with no external dependencies.
questions.answer: The worklet (function) inside useAnimatedStyle runs on the UI thread, which has no access to the JS runtime or React state. You CANNOT call JS-thread functions, access React hooks (useState, useContext), or console.log from inside useAnimatedStyle. You can ONLY: read shared values (.value), perform math, use Reanimated utilities (interpolate, clamp). Accessing regular React state from useAnimatedStyle causes a crash or unexpected behavior.
questions.explanation: If you need to use React state to control an animation: convert the state to a shared value with useEffect and useSharedValue. Then read the shared value in useAnimatedStyle. This bridges the JS and UI thread worlds correctly: state changes on JS thread → update shared value → useAnimatedStyle reacts on UI thread.

questions.question: What does Extrapolation.CLAMP do in interpolate(), and what happens without it?
options: CLAMP limits the animation to run once — without it, animations loop indefinitely.

CLAMP prevents the output value from going beyond the output range boundaries when the input exceeds the input range. Without CLAMP (default: EXTEND), if scrollY.value exceeds the inputRange max, the interpolated output continues extrapolating — a collapsible header height could become negative, and a title opacity could go below 0, potentially breaking the layout. CLAMP stops the value at the range boundary regardless of how far the input exceeds it.
CLAMP converts float animation values to integers for pixel-precise rendering.
Without CLAMP, the animation reverses direction when the input value exceeds the range.
questions.answer: CLAMP prevents the output value from going beyond the output range boundaries when the input exceeds the input range. Without CLAMP (default: EXTEND), if scrollY.value exceeds the inputRange max, the interpolated output continues extrapolating — a collapsible header height could become negative, and a title opacity could go below 0, potentially breaking the layout. CLAMP stops the value at the range boundary regardless of how far the input exceeds it.
questions.explanation: Example: interpolate(scrollY, [0, 100], [200, 60]) without CLAMP. When scrollY=200 (past the max), height extrapolates to 60 - 100*(200-60)/100 = -80px. Negative height crashes the layout. With CLAMP: height stays at 60 (the min) no matter how far scrollY exceeds 100.
